name: Ubaid Lizardmen Figurines
description: The Ubaid Lizardmen figurines are a collection of artifacts discovered at various archaeological sites in Mesopotamia, dating back to the Ubaid period (c. 6500-3800 BCE). These figurines, typically made of terracotta, depict humanoid figures with lizard-like features, such as elongated heads, slanted eyes, and reptilian faces. Proponents of alternative theories interpret these features as evidence of an ancient reptilian race that either ruled or influenced early human civilization. They suggest these beings may have been extraterrestrial or interdimensional entities. Mainstream archaeology views these figurines as representations of deities, priests, or possibly even stylized depictions of humans, emphasizing the symbolic and ritualistic significance of the figurines within Ubaid society. The reptilian features are often attributed to artistic conventions or symbolic representations of power, fertility, or other abstract concepts prevalent in the Ubaid culture. The lack of context for many finds and the diverse interpretations of ancient symbolism contributes to the ongoing debate. Critics emphasize the absence of concrete evidence supporting the existence of a reptilian race and highlight the dangers of misinterpreting ancient artifacts through a modern lens. They point to the broader diversity of Ubaid figurines, which include depictions of animals and other human-like figures, arguing that the "lizardmen" figurines are simply one variation among many.
subtitle: Ancient Mesopotamian artifacts sparking reptilian theories.

claims.claim: The figurines depict a reptilian race that influenced or ruled early Mesopotamian society.
claims.evidence: The elongated heads, slanted eyes, and reptilian facial features of the figurines.
claims.criticism: Mainstream archaeology interprets these features as stylized representations of deities, priests, or humans within the Ubaid culture's symbolic system. Artistic conventions are likely responsible for the 'reptilian' appearance.

claims.claim: The reptilian race was of extraterrestrial or interdimensional origin.
claims.evidence: The unusual features of the figurines suggest beings beyond human understanding and capabilities.
claims.criticism: There is no scientific evidence to support the existence of extraterrestrial or interdimensional beings influencing ancient Mesopotamia. The theory relies on speculation and misinterpretation of archaeological evidence.

claims.claim: The figurines are proof of a hidden history suppressed by mainstream academia.
claims.evidence: The lack of focus on the reptilian interpretation in mainstream archaeological publications.
claims.criticism: The reptilian interpretation is considered fringe due to a lack of supporting evidence and the existence of more plausible explanations rooted in established archaeological and anthropological understanding of Ubaid culture and symbolism.

keyFigures.name: David Icke
keyFigures.role: Prominent proponent of the reptilian conspiracy theory, incorporating the Ubaid figurines as evidence.

keyFigures.name: Susan Pollock
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist specializing in Mesopotamian history and culture, offering mainstream interpretations of Ubaid artifacts.

mythologyConnections: Possible connections to serpent deities in later Mesopotamian mythology
Speculative links to creation myths involving serpentine beings

timeline.event: The Ubaid period, during which the figurines were created.
The rise of alternative theories linking the figurines to reptilian conspiracies.
Continued debate and discussion regarding the interpretation of the figurines in both academic and alternative circles.

sources.author: Susan Pollock
sources.work: Ancient Mesopotamia: The Eden That Never Was
sources.title: Susan Pollock
